text: robotic transthoracic and transperitoneal esophagectomy on a da Vinci X system by Dr. Somshekhar SP, Head of the Department and Consultant Surgical Oncologist, Manipal Hospitals, Bangalore. A sixty-five-year-old male with ECOG of zero presented with dysphagia to solids more than liquid since one month There were no other complaints or comorbidities.

Upper GI scopy revealed a three by three centimeter growth in the lower one third of the esophagus with lumen being partially compromised. A CT scan confirmed the findings with no evidence of metastasis. Biopsy revealed it to be a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma grade two. Hence, patient was given two cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy with TPF regime.

After the workup, the plan was to do a total esophagectomy with two-field lymphadenectomy including the transthoracic and transperitoneal approach using the da Vinci egg system. Reconstruction was planned using the gastric tube pull-up with cervical anastomosis.

Intutu has an established procedure card for many surgical procedures on the da Vinci XI and SI system but for the esophageal transthoracic approach there were none. Hence we developed our own port placement technique for system X. Patient is given prone position with right arm completely hyper abducted and camera taken thirty degree down.

The robotic cart is positioned from the left shoulder side at an acute angle to the table's long axis. The camera port is inserted two intercostal spaces below the tip of the right scapula. The midline of the spine is marked, and R1 is placed seven centimeters from the midline, two to three intercostal spaces above the camera port, parallel to the medial border of the scapula. R2 is positioned two spaces below the camera port, seven centimeters from the midline. The assistant port is triangulated between the camera and arm two.

The surgeon uses hot shears on the right and a vessel sealer on the left to begin the dissection at the infra-azygos region. The inferior pulmonary ligament is incised, allowing the lung to be retracted downward. The esophagus is carefully separated from the underlying pericardium. The dissection progresses cranially to remove the subcarinal lymph nodes, identified as station VII.

The surgeon completes the removal of the subcarinal lymph nodes, revealing the left main bronchus. Dissection continues along the pericardium, maintaining clear visualization of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon employs the hanging from roof technique, keeping the esophagus attached to the roof to facilitate dissection. The thoracic duct is also dissected, and the vagus nerve is visualized at the caudal aspect.

The surgeon ligates the thoracic duct prophylactically to prevent iatrogenic injury during dissection of extensive esophageal lesions. The left pleura is visible, and the dissection proceeds caudally towards the lower gastroesophageal junction, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon performs dissection of the vagal nerve branches below the tracheal bifurcation, ensuring the proprioceptive branches are preserved to facilitate better cough reflex postoperatively.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the left bronchial artery. The procedure progresses towards the supra azygous region, with careful separation of tissues.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the right recurrent laryngeal group of lymph nodes in the mediastinum, carefully separating the lymph nodal packet from the surrounding tissue to avoid iatrogenic injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerves.

The surgeon exposes the membranous trachea during dissection, using hot shears cautiously to avoid iatrogenic injury. The assistant port is utilized to retract the trachea, ensuring safe manipulation in this delicate area.
The surgeon visualizes the subclavian vein during cranial dissection and routinely ligates the azygous vein. The esophagus is freed from the thoracic inlet down to the hiatus.
